Geese started as very young musicians in Brooklyn making ambitious rock records, and Getting Killed is the version of what they always seemed to be building toward. "Cobra" is proof that they got there.

posts.title: I've Seen It
posts.writeup:
"I've Seen It" is the kind of title that carries lived experience rather than description. Olivia Dean, at 25 when The Art of Loving came out in 2025, was writing with the authority of someone who had spent her years since the Messy Mercury Prize nomination actually living in the music and in the world. "I've Seen It" sounds like that: a song from the inside of an experience, not from outside observing it.

The production on this track is among the fuller sounds on the album, which makes sense given the title's declarative weight. There is nothing tentative about what the song is saying. Dean has earned the certainty she projects here through the specific way she builds the arrangement around the vocal rather than building the vocal around the arrangement.

A number-one UK album and a Grammy in the same year represents genuine arrival. "I've Seen It" is one of the reasons that arrival made sense to anyone paying attention.

posts.title: A Couple Minutes
posts.writeup:
Olivia Dean is from Hackney, London, and by 2025 she had moved from Mercury Prize-shortlisted debut to UK number-one album with The Art of Loving, which she described as an exploration of love in all its forms: romantic, familial, the craft of loving as a practice. "A Couple Minutes" sits in that frame: a song about what you can do with a small amount of time when you are present in it.

Her voice is one of the more immediately distinctive in UK pop: warm, clear, and precise in a way that comes from genuine classical training without sounding classical. She can do intimate and she can do large, and she knows which register each song requires. "A Couple Minutes" is a quieter moment on the record, which gives it a specific weight.

She won Best New Artist at the 68th Grammy Awards in February 2026. We had her on this page well before that. The moment was coming.

posts.title: Baby Steps
posts.writeup:
"Baby Steps" is Olivia Dean understanding that not all movement toward something has to be dramatic. The song is built on the recognition that incremental progress is still progress, which is a gentler and more human-scale idea than most love songs allow themselves. Her second album The Art of Loving is full of this kind of grounded observation: love as it actually operates rather than love as mythology.

She is from Hackney, studied at the Brit School, and carries that training in the precision of what she does. The Brit School has produced a lot of artists who overperform their emotional material. Dean has always been the opposite: she underplays, she trusts the writing, she lets the arrangement breathe. "Baby Steps" is that approach at its most deliberate.

The record went to number one in the UK and made her a Grammy winner. "Baby Steps" is part of why: it is a deceptively small song that does its work completely.

posts.title: Loud
posts.writeup:
"Loud" is one of the more energetic moments on The Art of Loving, and it serves a structural function: after some of the album's quieter and more introspective tracks, this one brings the temperature up and reminds you that Dean's voice has more dynamic range than the careful, controlled end of her catalog might suggest. She can be loud. This is her choosing to be.

She grew up in Hackney, went to the Brit School, and has consistently shown the versatility that institution tends to produce: she can do intimate and she can do arena-scale pop, and she knows when each approach is called for. "Loud" is the track where the arena end of her range gets its moment on an album that otherwise operates more quietly.

The UK number-one album, the Grammy win, the BRIT and Mercury recognition over multiple years: Olivia Dean's trajectory has been remarkable. "Loud" is her making sure the second album is not just critically appreciated but felt in a room. It works.

posts.title: Something Inbetween
posts.writeup:
"Something Inbetween" is Olivia Dean in the space her album title describes: the art of loving is not only the clearly defined states, the fully in or fully out, but the complicated middle ground that most pop songs pretend does not exist. She has always been better than her peers at writing from inside ambiguity rather than resolving it too quickly, and this track is a clean example.

The Hackney-born, Brit School-trained singer has said that she is a hopelessly romantic person who is also clear-eyed about what love actually requires. "Something Inbetween" holds both of those things: the romanticism and the realism, in the same verse, without either canceling the other out.

The album went to number one in the UK. The Grammy for Best New Artist followed in February 2026. We were paying attention well before both of those things, and songs like this one are exactly why.

posts.title: Let Alone The One You Love
posts.writeup:
The grammar of this title is doing specific work: "let alone" as a phrase means a step further, a harder version of something already assumed. You cannot do the smaller thing, let alone this larger one. Olivia Dean uses language with a songwriter's precision, and "Let Alone The One You Love" is a title that rewards the attention she pays to it.

The track is in the more introspective register of what The Art of Loving does. The album is not uniformly upbeat: it is an honest exploration of how loving actually operates, which means the harder moments exist alongside the warmer ones. "Let Alone The One You Love" is one of the moments where the album asks something difficult.

Dean has talked about love as a craft, something you practice rather than just feel. Songs like this one are what that understanding produces: not the feeling of love described from the outside, but the work of it from the inside.

posts.title: So Easy (To Fall In Love)
posts.writeup:
Olivia Dean talked about "So Easy (To Fall In Love)" in an NPR interview from September 2025, in which she described the song as being about knowing her own worth: here is what I offer, here is the space I can give you, and if you want that, come find me. It is a love song that knows exactly what it is worth, which is rarer in the format than it should be.

The song is from The Art of Loving, the album she described as an argument that love is a craft rather than just a feeling. That framing is consistent with everything she does: the writing is deliberate, the vocal is controlled, and the production gives her room to be specific without filling every corner with sound.

Her mother and aunt, whom she has cited as strong, independent women who shaped how she thinks about relationships, are present in the DNA of this song. It sounds like someone who was raised to know the difference between settling and choosing.

posts.title: HARDHEAD
posts.writeup:
La Reezy is a 20-something rapper, songwriter, and producer out of New Orleans who has been accumulating serious cosigns from established figures in the rap world over the past couple of years. "HARDHEAD" was produced by Khayree Sanders and released as part of his LAREEZYANA SHAKEDOWN project in September 2025. It is exactly what the title promises: stubborn, confident, New Orleans through every bar.

New Orleans has always had its own relationship to rap that does not track perfectly with what is happening in Houston, Atlanta, or New York. The bounce tradition, the brass band DNA, the particular energy of a city that rebuilt itself: all of it shows up in how artists from there approach the music. La Reezy carries that regional identity without making it the whole story. He is from the city, not a tourist in it.

He is self-made and operates with the focus of someone who has been building toward this moment for a while. "HARDHEAD" is a strong argument for why people should be paying attention.

posts.title: Autobody
posts.writeup:
Connor Creasey makes the kind of music that rewards close listening without demanding it. "Autobody," his collaboration with RUTHE, has an easiness to it that takes work to construct: the production sounds unhurried, the writing is unforced, and the two voices sit naturally against each other without competing for space.

Creasey operates in an indie-adjacent lane where the craft tends to be understated and the songs speak for themselves. "Autobody" is a good example of what that approach can produce when the raw material is strong: a track that does not announce itself but keeps returning.

RUTHE brings a textural quality that deepens the song without shifting its weight. The collaboration feels like two people who understood what each other needed before they started recording. We like the result.

posts.title: Miss My Dogs
posts.writeup: Young Thug in an emotional register he doesn't always show in the flashiest work. "Miss My Dogs" is sincere and a little raw, the production giving him space rather than spectacle. It's a reminder that the range has always been there, you just have to catch him in the right moment.

posts.title: bleach
posts.writeup:
"Bleach" is the opening track on Bakar's BEASTIE EP and functions as a statement of intent. For a project that gets progressively more atmospheric and experimental toward its end, opening with something direct and sonically grounded is a choice that pays off: you get the contrast only once you have already committed to following him somewhere less familiar.

Bakar is London born, Nigerian by heritage, and his sound has always carried that dual inheritance. British indie rock on the surface, something harder to categorize underneath. BEASTIE is him operating with more confidence in that hybridity than before. Reviews consistently singled out "bleach" as a highlight, the track that made the strongest case for the direction he is building toward.

We think the tension between where "bleach" starts and where "Hey Doc" ends is actually the whole point of the record. But as a standalone song, it holds up completely on its own terms.

posts.title: Not Like That Anymore
posts.writeup:
Lola Young is from Greenwich, South London, and her voice has the kind of quality that makes you stop what you are doing when it comes on. She released "Messy" in 2023 and it gave her a profile that her earlier EPs had been building toward for years. By 2025, she was operating with the confidence of someone who had already survived the difficult middle period between early attention and real establishment.

"Not Like That Anymore" is a song about change that does not pretend the change was easy. Young has always written with more specificity than her age would suggest, and her approach here is exactly that: here is what I was, here is what I am not anymore, here is the gap between those two things. The production gives her room to let the feeling land.

She has compared her influences to Amy Winehouse and neo-soul artists of the early 2000s, and you can hear that lineage. But she sounds like no one else. "Not Like That Anymore" is a good argument for why that matters.

posts.title: nobody (make me feel)
posts.writeup:
"nobody (make me feel)" is an Oslo-to-El Paso collaboration: oskar med k is a Norwegian producer, and Khalid is from El Paso, Texas, by way of a military family background that moved him to Fort Bliss at 17. They should not obviously work together on paper, but the track lands because Khalid has always been better at restraint than his mainstream profile suggests, and oskar med k's production gives him the right amount of space to be exactly that.

The song arrived in September 2025 and plugged into Khalid's After the Sun Goes Down campaign. Khalid has been one of the more careful builders of a pop career in the past decade: the debut was enormous, the subsequent projects have been more selective, and "nobody (make me feel)" is him choosing a collaboration that serves the writing rather than the moment.

The hook lands because it earns it. Both artists know when to stop adding and let the core of the song be heard. That is rarer than it looks.

posts.title: Crazy Eyes
posts.writeup:
Morgan Wallen is from Sneedville, Tennessee, a town of fewer than 1,500 people, and has become the biggest name in country music by not pretending to be anything else. I'm the Problem, released May 2025 via Big Loud, Mercury, and Republic Records, sold 133,000 copies in its first week and gave him the fourth-largest sales week of any album across all genres that year. "Crazy Eyes" is part of that record: the kind of track that fills out an album built for people who will listen all the way through.

The album takes its title from something Wallen has said about himself, which is part of the operation: he is better than most commercial country artists at writing about his own flaws with enough honesty that the audience believes him. "Crazy Eyes" fits that mode, describing the particular intensity of someone who is all the way in.

We cover Morgan Wallen because the music earns it, full stop. Whatever the surrounding conversation, the songs hold up. "Crazy Eyes" is a good example of that.

posts.title: LA Night
posts.writeup: Morgan Wallen trading Tennessee for the Pacific in 'LA Night' — the production leans into the switch, all shimmer and low-end warmth where his country roots usually plant themselves. He sounds at home in the blur between genres, and the chorus earns it. Worth your time.

posts.title: Baby Blue (feat. Oliver Sim)
posts.writeup: SG Lewis has been quietly building one of the more consistent catalogs in UK electronic music, and 'Baby Blue' with Oliver Sim is a proper showcase of why. Sim's vocals carry the melancholy; Lewis wraps it in disco-adjacent production that doesn't try too hard. Keep an ear on this pairing.

posts.title: A COLD PLAY
posts.writeup: The Kid LAROI finds a middle ground between emo-pop and hard-edged rap on 'A COLD PLAY' — the title is a flex and the track backs it up. Punchy production, vocal control that's grown noticeably since his debut run. We've been watching this one develop.

posts.title: Sideways
posts.writeup: Geneva-based Balu Brigada have been inching toward something special for a while, and 'Sideways' delivers on that promise. Warm synths, effortless groove, the kind of song that sounds like late summer without announcing it. Right up your alley if Jungle or Parcels are in rotation.

posts.title: Space
posts.writeup: Zach John King carves out room with 'Space' — hushed vocals over production that breathes, nothing overcrowded. The simplicity is the point. An artist worth tracking early.

posts.title: Carolina in My Mind
posts.writeup: Dominique Way's take on the James Taylor classic respects the source without disappearing into it — the arrangement is spare and the vocal has enough grit to make it feel personal. A cover that earns its existence.

posts.title: Speed Of Love (feat. Jazmine Sullivan)
posts.writeup: Chance and Jazmine Sullivan shouldn't need an introduction at this point. 'Speed Of Love' is gospel-inflected and unhurried — two voices that know exactly what they're doing, circling each other without competing. This is what patience sounds like in a collab.

posts.title: Just A Drop (feat. Jay Electronica)
posts.artist: Chance the Rapper, J A Y E L E C T R O N I C A
posts.writeup: Jay Electronica is still one of the most mythologized figures in rap, and every time he surfaces it means something. 'Just A Drop' with Chance leans spiritual and dense — both artists in their element, the production giving them room to move. Feast your ears.

posts.title: Different Species (feat. Gunna)
posts.writeup: Offset and Gunna are operating in their natural habitat — melodic trap with production that hits right. 'Different Species' doesn't reinvent anything, but it doesn't need to. These two elevate familiar territory.

posts.title: Love You Down
posts.writeup: Offset goes softer on 'Love You Down,' showing range that doesn't always get highlighted in the bigger single rollouts. The track breathes more than his usual output, and it suits him.

posts.title: Wolves of Worry
posts.writeup: Chance Peña is one of the more underrated voices in the current indie-folk space — 'Wolves of Worry' leans into his gift for understated storytelling, anxious and honest without being overwrought. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: PAY ME (feat. Stormzy and Zlatan)
posts.writeup: ODUMODUBLVCK pulling Stormzy and Nigerian rap royalty Zlatan onto the same record is a statement. 'PAY ME' moves with confidence through Afrobeats-meets-UK energy — the kind of track that proves Lagos and London are having the same conversation. We've been watching ODUMODUBLVCK's trajectory closely.

posts.title: Politix
posts.writeup: Balu Brigada show another side on 'Politix' — slightly darker production, the groove still there but with some edge underneath. A good complement to 'Sideways' if you're building a playlist.

posts.title: There For You
posts.writeup: Lecrae and Fridayy find common ground on 'There For You' — faith-forward without being insular, Fridayy's melodic instincts pulling it toward something that crosses over naturally. Warm production, genuine sentiment.

posts.title: Rock A Bye Baby
posts.writeup: Jean Dawson is one of the few artists making music that genuinely defies easy categorization — 'Rock A Bye Baby' sits in that liminal space between noise-rock and R&B that he's carved out on his own terms. Disorienting in the best way.

posts.title: loyal & marie
posts.writeup: Dijon is one of the more emotionally precise songwriters working right now, and 'loyal & marie' is vintage him — bare arrangements, plain-spoken devotion, production that knows when to disappear. The kind of track that ages well.

posts.title: Man I Need
posts.writeup: Olivia Dean's soul-pop instincts are fully realized on 'Man I Need' — the production has that classic Motown warmth filtered through something contemporary, and her voice does exactly what it needs to. London's got a real one here.

posts.title: Gun In Yo Purse (feat. Young Thug & TiaCorine)
posts.artist: Chance the Rapper, Young Thug, TiaCorine
posts.writeup: Chance bringing in Young Thug and TiaCorine on the same record is chaotic in the right way — 'Gun In Yo Purse' is loose and playful, everyone doing their thing without crowding the track. TiaCorine's hook is the anchor.

posts.title: Link Me In The Future
posts.writeup: Introspective Chance is always worth the listen. 'Link Me In The Future' has that spiritual optimism he's worn as a signature since Acid Rap — sparse production, direct sentiment, no safety net.

posts.title: Ride (feat. Do or Die)
posts.artist: Chance the Rapper, Do Or Die
posts.writeup: Chance looping in Chicago legacy act Do or Die is a flex with roots attached. 'Ride' nods to the city's history without feeling like a museum piece — it moves.

posts.title: FOOLS GOLD
posts.writeup: SLOE JACK dropping 'FOOLS GOLD' and it's exactly the kind of track that earned them the Position Music deal. Dark-edged pop with enough grit underneath to separate it from the rest of the pile. We've been watching these guys since day one and the ceiling keeps moving up.

posts.title: leaving the light on
posts.writeup: Siena's 'leaving the light on' is patient, restrained indie-pop with a vocal that doesn't oversell the emotion. The production stays out of the way and lets the song breathe. An artist worth tracking early.

posts.title: spaghetti stains
posts.writeup: Siena keeps the run going with 'spaghetti stains' — a slightly more playful entry but still rooted in the same understated aesthetic. The specificity of the title tells you something about her writing.

posts.title: swimming
posts.writeup: Back-to-back with Siena is warranted — 'swimming' has that same instinct for space, the arrangement minimal and the feeling specific. She's building a catalog with real coherence.

posts.title: the end
posts.writeup: Siena closes out her run with 'the end,' and it lands. Quiet, direct, the kind of song that earns a last-track placement. Feast your ears on this whole sequence — she's one to watch.

posts.title: Wholeheartedly (with Ty Dolla $ign & 6LACK)
posts.writeup: JID brings Ty Dolla $ign and 6LACK into his orbit on this slow-burning standout from *God Does Like Ugly*. The production breathes low and warm, letting all three voices find their lane without crowding each other. JID stays sharp even at a lullaby tempo, and 6LACK's closing stretch is the kind of thing you replay twice before moving on. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Lose My Mind
posts.writeup: Tolou works in that space between indie pop and something rawer, and "Lose My Mind" finds her at her most direct. The production is spare enough to let the vocal do the work, and she doesn't oversell it. An artist worth watching.

posts.title: Falling For The Feeling
posts.writeup: Hether leans into the push-pull of new attraction without tipping into cliche. "Falling For The Feeling" has a clean, radio-ready structure but there's enough texture in the production to keep it interesting. She's got the voice to go somewhere.

posts.title: Know You Naked
posts.writeup: LANY doing what LANY does best: late-night emotional territory with production that stays just bright enough to keep it from sinking. "Know You Naked" lands in the sweet spot of the catalog. If you've been a fan, this is exactly what you came back for.

posts.title: Is It a Crime
posts.writeup: Mariah the Scientist and Kali Uchis together is a flex that delivers. "Is It a Crime" has the kind of tension that makes R&B worth following right now. Two artists at the top of their craft, neither one playing second chair. This one travels.

posts.title: coming around
posts.writeup: UK artist fliss has been quietly building something, and "coming around" is the clearest look yet. The songwriting is precise without being cold, and the production has a warmth that sneaks up on you. Right up your alley if you've been sleeping on the British indie-pop underground.

posts.title: buttrock
posts.writeup: boylife's *jones* is a strange and honest record, and "buttrock" captures its weirder side. No preview available, but the album is worth sitting with. This is music that resists easy categorization and doesn't apologize for it.

posts.title: junior
posts.writeup: The more accessible side of *jones*, "junior" still has boylife's signature off-kilter emotional logic. Warm, a little bruised, and more melodic than most of what's around it. We've been watching this project closely.

posts.title: cartilage
posts.writeup: Ethan Regan makes quiet music that hits harder than it should. "cartilage" off *honey honey honey* has the kind of delicate production that rewards headphones. If you know, you know.

posts.title: Had To Be A Cowboy
posts.writeup: XOLEX plays it straight on "Had To Be A Cowboy" and it works. Country DNA with enough contemporary production sensibility to reach across genres. Low streaming numbers, high ceiling.

posts.title: happy ending
posts.writeup: Anna Clendening keeps the arrangement clean and lets the lyric land. "happy ending" is the kind of stripped-back pop that doesn't need tricks. She's been at this long enough to know what works.

posts.title: To Be Free
posts.writeup: Sam Smith strips it back to voice and intention on "To Be Free." At its core this is a showcase for one of the best instruments in pop music. No frills, no overcrowding. It lands.

posts.title: d£aler
posts.writeup: Lola Young is having a moment and "d£aler" explains exactly why. The production is controlled chaos, and her vocal sits right on top of it without breaking a sweat. UK's real one. Feast your ears.

posts.title: Which One (feat. Central Cee)
posts.writeup: Drake and Central Cee share a frequency on "Which One" that sounds natural rather than forced. The UK/Toronto handshake feels earned. Leaning minimal on the production, letting the chemistry carry it. Works.

posts.title: Life Behind Bars
posts.writeup: Zach John King and Bayker Blankenship find a groove on "Life Behind Bars" that sits somewhere between outlaw country and modern Americana. The songwriting is lived-in and the production doesn't overcomplicate what the song is already doing well.

posts.title: d.b.a.b
posts.writeup: Jim Legxacy is one of the sharper voices out of the UK scene right now, and *black british music (2025)* is a statement record. "d.b.a.b" doesn't have a preview, but don't let that stop you from digging in.

posts.title: The Man That Came Back
posts.writeup: Jessie Murph continues to blur the country/rock line in ways that feel honest rather than strategic. "The Man That Came Back" has real emotional weight, and her voice carries it without oversinging. *Sex Hysteria* is a bold record.

posts.title: Ain't But a Thing
posts.writeup: The more immediate side of *Sex Hysteria*, "Ain't But a Thing" shows Jessie Murph can play it lean when she wants to. Tight, confident, zero wasted space.

posts.title: Monkey See
posts.writeup: Rosier, Peterparker69, and tom huna stack up something interesting on "Monkey See." The collab energy is real and the track moves in ways you don't always see coming. Worth a listen if you dig the DIY end of the alt-pop spectrum.

posts.title: crickets!!!
posts.writeup: hard life's "crickets!!!" hits that anxious energy sweet spot, production that's all nervous energy and the vocal rides it perfectly. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: let me go
posts.writeup: woody keeps it almost uncomfortably intimate on "let me go." Bedroom production, confessional lyric, and a kind of rawness that you don't engineer. Early days but the instincts are there.

posts.title: Happy Ever After You
posts.artist: MacKenzie Porter, Jake Etheridge, Thelma & James
posts.writeup: MacKenzie Porter has been one of the steadiest voices in country-adjacent pop and "Happy Ever After You" is a well-crafted closer. The production is lush without being excessive and the emotion is earned.

posts.title: Dream Therapy (feat. Sampha)
posts.writeup: Oscar Farrell brings in Sampha and the result is exactly as considered as you'd hope. "Dream Therapy" moves slowly and deliberately, two artists operating at a frequency most can't reach. This one deserves attention.

posts.title: Spin the Bottle
posts.writeup: Eli Patterson is brand new and "Spin the Bottle" reads like a genuine first impression. Rough around the edges in the right way. Popularity of 1 means you're early.

posts.title: Blue Ridge Run
posts.writeup: Hunter Metts has a voice built for open spaces and "Blue Ridge Run" gives it room to move. Americana with real emotional stakes and no filler. Right up your alley.

posts.title: Way Of The World (feat. Ella Langley)
posts.writeup: Noah Cyrus and Ella Langley trade verses across a track that leans into ache rather than spectacle. The production stays sparse, piano and brushed percussion giving both vocalists room to breathe. Two voices that know how to sit with weight instead of perform it.

posts.title: mangetout
posts.writeup: Wet Leg keep the wry Isle of Wight edge intact on mangetout while pushing toward something slightly more textured. The guitar work carries that familiar dry bite, but the melodic payoff feels more considered this time. Still playful, still precise.

posts.title: Change Your Mind (feat. Shaboozey)
posts.writeup: Burna Boy and Shaboozey find more common ground than you might expect. The Afrobeats backbone bends toward country soul somewhere in the middle, and Shaboozey sounds completely at home in the pocket. A pairing that makes sense the moment it hits.

posts.title: hollow
posts.writeup: zachy operates in the space between bedroom pop and alt-folk with hollow, building tension slowly over fingerpicked guitar and a vocal that stays understated throughout. Keep an ear on this one. The restraint is doing a lot of heavy lifting.

posts.title: YUKON
posts.writeup: YUKON is one of the quieter cuts from the recent run, drifting through ambient textures and sparse vocals with minimal pop polish. It sounds like someone working something out in real time, and that rawness is the point.

posts.title: TOO LONG
posts.writeup: TOO LONG stretches into more introspective territory, the production loose and unhurried. Bieber leans into the discomfort rather than resolving it, which keeps it more interesting than most recent output.

posts.title: DEVOTION
posts.writeup: Dijon on DEVOTION elevates the whole thing. His harmonic sensibility pulls Bieber somewhere more soulful, and the result is one of the more emotionally honest moments on the project. Right up your alley if you lean neo-soul.

posts.title: WALKING AWAY
posts.writeup: WALKING AWAY sits in a slow-burn R&B groove, minimal production letting the vocal carry the weight. Not flashy, but it lands. Bieber sounds more settled here than he has in years.

posts.title: WAY IT IS
posts.writeup: Gunna slides in naturally on WAY IT IS, his cadence sitting loose over a trap-inflected beat that Bieber rides with more confidence than most of this record. One of the more direct cuts from the drop.

posts.title: GO BABY
posts.writeup: GO BABY is the closest thing to a banger in the batch, the energy a few degrees warmer and the tempo inviting. Short, punchy, and earwormy in the right way.

posts.title: DAISIES
posts.writeup: DAISIES trades in soft acoustic warmth, the kind of track that feels built for 6 AM or late nights when the noise is gone. Bieber is clearly comfortable in this register, and the simplicity works.

posts.title: So Far Ahead
posts.artist: Clipse, Pharrell Williams, Pusha T, Malice
posts.writeup: Clipse back with Pharrell producing and both brothers sharp as ever. So Far Ahead moves with that classic Re-Up swagger, coke rap precision intact, every bar placed with intent. We have been waiting for this.

posts.title: F.I.C.O.
posts.artist: Clipse, Stove God Cooks, Pusha T, Malice
posts.writeup: F.I.C.O. brings in Stove God Cooks, whose pen fits the Clipse framework like it was always supposed to be there. Pusha and Malice trade verses with clinical efficiency. The production is lean and menacing.

posts.title: So Be It
posts.writeup: Shorter and blunter than some of the other cuts, So Be It is Clipse doing what they have always done best: controlled aggression over immaculate beats. No wasted motion.

posts.title: Chains & Whips
posts.artist: Clipse, Kendrick Lamar, Pharrell Williams, Pusha T, Malice
posts.writeup: Kendrick on a Clipse track demands your full attention. Chains and Whips delivers on that promise. Pharrell production hits differently when you have this much pen talent stacked on it.

posts.title: The Birds Don't Sing
posts.artist: Clipse, John Legend, Voices of Fire, Pusha T, Malice
posts.writeup: The most ambitious swing on the project. John Legend and the Voices of Fire choir bring a gospel weight that contrasts hard against Pusha and Malice delivery. It should not work on paper and it works completely.

posts.title: tony soprano.
posts.writeup: Brent Faiyaz commanding the room on tony soprano. Slow, deliberate, built on bass and restraint. His vocal performance is dripping in confidence, and the beat gives him exactly the room he needs.

posts.title: Oneida
posts.writeup: Tyler Childers tapping into something older and more elemental on Oneida. The storytelling is grounded in place and feeling, his voice carrying the kind of grit that does not come from performance. Eastern Kentucky soul.

posts.title: River Washed Hair
posts.writeup: Zach Bryan in his naturalist bag. River Washed Hair is pastoral and unhurried, acoustic-led and image-heavy in the way his best writing tends to be. Sounds like something you would hear through a screen door in July.

posts.title: What Did I Miss?
posts.writeup: Drake reappearing with the kind of self-aware title that either lands or does not depending on your patience. Sonically it is clean and confident, the production sitting in that familiar moody lane. Whether he actually missed anything is a separate conversation.

posts.title: I'm The Problem
posts.writeup: Morgan Wallen turning self-reflection into a mid-tempo country groove. I am The Problem does not overcorrect into apology, it just sits with accountability in a way that feels more honest than redemption-arc country usually does.

posts.title: Just In Case
posts.writeup: Just In Case is the softer side of the run, vulnerability without the melodrama. Wallen voice in this lower-key register has always been his strongest card.

posts.title: FEEL SOMETHING
posts.writeup: Sasha Keable has been one of the more underrated voices in UK R&B and FEEL SOMETHING is a reminder of exactly why. Her vocal control is exceptional and the production does not crowd it. Feast your ears.

posts.title: Bet
posts.writeup: Hana Eid with a confident debut statement. Bet is sleek and self-possessed, minimalist production letting her voice and attitude carry the whole track. Keep an ear on where this one goes.

posts.title: y3llow bike
posts.writeup: hard life delivering something with genuine heart on y3llow bike. The lo-fi tag undersells it. There is a melodic instinct here that sticks around after the song ends. Worth watching.

posts.title: Philadelphia
posts.writeup: Clare Perrott opens with a piano figure that sounds like rain on a window and never lets go. The UK singer-songwriter keeps the production sparse enough to let every syllable land, and her voice has this quiet authority that earns the emotional weight. Philadelphia is the kind of song that sneaks up on you.

posts.title: Victory Lap
posts.writeup: Fred again.. brings Skepta and Plaqueboymax together on a track that hits harder than it has any right to at this tempo. The production stays liquid but grounds itself with a bass thump that keeps the energy locked. Skepta sounds reinvigorated, Plaqueboymax delivers, and Fred ties it all together. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: BEAUTY AND THE BEAST
posts.writeup: Ye in full chaos mode, and it works. BEAUTY AND THE BEAST leans into a cinematic gospel sample with enough distortion to feel unhinged, which is exactly the point. Whatever you think about the man, the musical instincts are still there.

posts.title: PREACHER MAN
posts.writeup: PREACHER MAN goes deeper into the gospel well, building around a choir swell that Ye rides with more restraint than expected. The production pacing is the real story here: it breathes, then it doesn't.

posts.title: I'm in Love
posts.writeup: S.G. Goodman writes from the Kentucky soil and you can feel it. I'm in Love is a slow-burning country soul cut with a voice that sounds like it's been lived in. She's one of the more underrated storytellers working right now.

posts.title: 6 Months Later
posts.writeup: Megan Moroney keeps proving she's one of country's sharpest writers. 6 Months Later is a breakup song that skips the theatrics and goes straight for the gut with specifics. The production stays clean and lets the lyric do the work.

posts.title: Ace Trumpets
posts.writeup: Clipse back in formation, and Pusha T sounds like he never left. Ace Trumpets brings a brass-forward sample flipped into something cold and precise, the kind of rap record that rewards repeat listens. Malice holds his own. We've been waiting for this.

posts.title: West Life
posts.writeup: AJ Tracey takes the UK drill template and softens just the edges enough to let some melody breathe through. West Life has a nostalgic pull without being overly sentimental. He's one of the few who can pull that balance off.

posts.title: Distant Strangers (feat. The Kid LAROI & Imogen Heap)
posts.artist: ONEFOUR, The Kid LAROI, Imogen Heap
posts.writeup: Australian drill outfit ONEFOUR links with The Kid LAROI and Imogen Heap on a track that shouldn't work as cleanly as it does. Heap's presence anchors the emotional center while LAROI and ONEFOUR handle the grit. Unexpected and genuinely good.

posts.title: Red Wine (feat. Master Peace)
posts.writeup: AJ Tracey and Master Peace make Red Wine feel effortless. The groove is loose but the production detail is tight, and both artists know exactly how much to give at any given moment. Right up your alley if you've been following either.

posts.title: Keep On Keeping On
posts.writeup: Tommy WA delivers a rooted, unhurried pop track that leans into warmth over flash. Keep On Keeping On sounds like something you'd discover at the bottom of a Spotify rabbit hole and immediately save. Quiet confidence all the way through.

posts.title: Geezer
posts.writeup: Kevin Abstract and Dominic Fike together is the kind of pairing that makes sense the second you hear it. Geezer floats on a hazy production bed with both artists sounding loose and unbothered. The chemistry is real.

posts.title: I'm A Little Crazy
posts.writeup: Morgan Wallen does what he does best: finds the line between self-deprecation and charm and parks right on it. I'm A Little Crazy is a country confessional that feels lived-in, not workshopped. Hard not to like.

posts.title: TN
posts.writeup: TN is Wallen in love letter mode for his home state. The production is big enough for stadium country but the emotion stays personal. Three Wallen tracks in this batch is no accident; he's in a serious run right now.

posts.title: Welcome to Tha Carter
posts.writeup: Lil Wayne kicking the door open on Tha Carter VI with a scene-setter that reminds you exactly who you're dealing with. The flow is still elastic, the confidence still absolute. Welcome back.

posts.title: Crying Over Nothing
posts.writeup: Indigo De Souza has a gift for making emotional honesty feel musical rather than confessional. Crying Over Nothing rides a slow-building arrangement that earns its catharsis. The Asheville singer-songwriter keeps getting better.

posts.title: FATHER FIGURE
posts.writeup: Jon Bellion goes somewhere more personal on FATHER FIGURE. The production is bigger than usual but the lyric anchors it, and his voice carries the weight without straining. A career highlight.

posts.title: Cruel Love
posts.writeup: Leon Vynehall and Beau Nox build Cruel Love on a foundation of UK club production that refuses to rush. The restraint is the point: every element earns its place, and the emotional undercurrent hits harder for it.

posts.title: ALL I WANT IS YOU
posts.writeup: The Kid LAROI taps back into the melodic instincts that made him a name to watch in the first place. ALL I WANT IS YOU is lean and direct, with a hook that lands on first listen. No overthinking, just results.

posts.title: vain
posts.writeup: Chesle keeps it minimal and moody on vain. The production leaves a lot of space and the vocal sits right in it. Not much context on this one yet, but the track speaks for itself.

posts.title: 20 Cigarettes
posts.writeup: 20 Cigarettes is Wallen at his most nostalgic. The production shimmers in a way that feels late-night and regretful, and the specificity of the imagery is what makes it stick. Classic formula, executed well.

posts.title: Crash Out
posts.writeup: Lihtz brings some edge to the queue. Crash Out is built on a propulsive drum pattern with production that presses on the chest a little. New name to us, worth watching.

posts.title: Gimmicks
posts.writeup: GRACEY strips the excess and goes straight for the song on Gimmicks. The UK pop artist has been building quietly for a while, and this track shows exactly why the patience has paid off. Clear-eyed and precise.

posts.title: Backseat
posts.writeup: Balu Brigada specializes in indie pop with just enough texture to keep it interesting. Backseat has a momentum to it, a hook that feels earned, and production that doesn't overstay its welcome.

posts.title: Nice To Each Other
posts.writeup: Olivia Dean closes this batch with the kind of song that makes everything feel a little more manageable. Nice To Each Other is warm, direct, and produced with just enough soul influence to give it body. She's one to keep close.

posts.title: OWA OWA
posts.writeup:
Lil Tecca broke through in 2019 when he was 17 years old with "Ransom," one of the most efficient breakout singles of that era: short, melodic, confident, and immediately recognizable. He is from Queens and Jamaica, New York, and has spent the years since proving that the early attention was not accidental. "OWA OWA" is him in a register that fans of the melodic, bouncy end of his catalog will recognize immediately.

The title is a sound before it is a phrase, which is consistent with what Tecca does well: he understands that melody and rhythm carry more persuasive weight than lyrical complexity in this lane, and he does not pretend otherwise. The hook arrives and does exactly what it is supposed to do.

He was 23 when this released, with multiple years of professional experience and a clear sense of what he is making and why. "OWA OWA" is him operating from that settled understanding: nothing to prove, everything still to do.

posts.title: get by
posts.writeup:
Ethan Regan was born in Raleigh and raised in Charlotte, and by the time he was attending Penn State, he had already been teaching himself production on Ableton since high school. He chose music over a more predictable path, started posting twice a day on TikTok, and eventually "Durham" caught a viral moment that translated into millions of Spotify streams. "My Fault" and "Secrecy" followed the same trajectory.

"get by" comes from his 2025 EP honey honey honey, and it sits in the more introspective lane of what he does. Regan's approach is genuinely hybrid: folk songwriting underneath, production instincts shaped by hip-hop and electronic textures, vulnerability that does not read as performed. The lowercase titling is consistent with a general refusal to dress things up.

He is self-produced, self-contained, and building something that feels earned rather than manufactured. "get by" is a good entry point for understanding why people keep coming back.

posts.title: Won't Be Lonely
posts.writeup:
Fredo Bang has been one of Baton Rouge's most consistent voices in street rap for the better part of the past decade, and "Won't Be Lonely" represents the more emotionally open register he is increasingly willing to work in. It came out as part of The Big Bang, his 2025 EMPIRE album, and sits as one of the more tender moments on a project that otherwise operates at a higher temperature.

The Baton Rouge scene has always produced artists who understand the relationship between hardness and vulnerability in a way that a lot of rap does not. Fredo Bang comes from that tradition. "Won't Be Lonely" is him extending that understanding further than he usually does: the production gives him room to breathe, and he takes it.

This is not the track that was going to rack up the biggest numbers off The Big Bang, but it might be the one that holds up longest. Songs that take a risk on being this direct about loneliness tend to find their audience eventually.

posts.title: WHY (feat. Luke Combs)
posts.writeup:
Jon Bellion wrote "WHY" in the 48 hours before his first son was born. That context does not require extra explanation: everything in the song earns that framing, and you can hear it. The production is pulled back compared to some of his more maximalist work, and the writing is direct in a way that songs written from that particular kind of suspended-before-the-rest-of-your-life clarity tend to be.

Bringing in Luke Combs was a genuine surprise on paper, but it makes complete sense on the track. Combs is from Asheville, North Carolina, and carries an emotional directness in his voice that matches what the song is asking for. He does not try to out-country the track or out-pop Bellion. He just shows up and sings it, which is exactly right.

"WHY" arrived in May 2025 and landed immediately with people who were ready for it. Songs about what it means to have someone depend on you, written from a place of genuine feeling rather than calculated sentiment, tend to travel. This one traveled.

posts.title: FINALLY
posts.writeup:
"FINALLY" is the kind of title that comes at the end of a long road rather than the beginning of one. Lihtz has been putting in work long enough to earn the word: the West Philly upbringing, the difficult early years, the Future cosign, and then the patient construction of a catalog that operates on his own terms rather than the market's. By 2025, "FINALLY" reads as a statement.

The track is all caps, which in Lihtz's context functions as a sonic instruction as much as a design choice. His rap energy is typically deliberate and considered, but the all-caps titles signal something with more heat in it. "FINALLY" delivers on that: this is him with the pressure turned up rather than modulated.

Philadelphia has produced some of the most distinctive rap voices in the country for several decades. Lihtz belongs in that lineage while not sounding like any of his predecessors. That is the whole point.

posts.title: SERENITY
posts.writeup:
"SERENITY" in an all-caps Lihtz context is not what the word usually signals. There is something almost confrontational about naming a track "SERENITY" when the artist's body of work has always involved processing difficulty rather than avoiding it. But that contrast is the whole argument: serenity earned through hardship is something different from serenity that has never been tested.

Lihtz grew up watching his mother move through illness, moved between shelters as a child, and built a career from a neighborhood that does not produce rap stars on any kind of consistent basis. The West Oak Lane area of Philadelphia is not Compton or Harlem. It is its own specific thing, and his music reflects that specificity.

In 2025, "SERENITY" represents something the music has been building toward: the possibility of stillness after everything that preceded it. We think it earns the title.

posts.title: Anemic
posts.writeup:
Lihtz grew up in West Philly, moved with his family through shelters before settling in the West Oak Lane area of the city. His mother was sick. The origin story is difficult, and it is present in his music without being performed as content. When Future personally selected him for the 1800 Seconds Vol. 2 compilation in 2020, it put a bigger spotlight on what Philly already knew: that this was a serious artist with a specific perspective.

"Anemic" works the way Lihtz works at his best: he takes a word that describes something depleted, drained of what it needs, and applies it to an emotional or relational context that carries all that weight. His production sense and his lyrical sense are aligned. Nothing in his music is accidental.

He has been building steadily with 258K monthly listeners on Spotify without a major label infrastructure behind him. "Anemic" is a good example of what that kind of focused, independent work produces over time.

posts.title: RIOT
posts.writeup:
If "FINALLY" signals an arrival, "RIOT" signals what that arrival costs. Lihtz has never made comfortable music, and a track titled "RIOT" is not going to start. West Philly, the shelter years, the grinding path to the Future cosign and beyond: the backstory feeds the music, and "RIOT" is the version of Lihtz where that energy is at its least contained.

The all-caps titling across several of his 2025 releases was a consistent signal: there is something happening here that is bigger and louder than the understated delivery of some of his earlier work. He can do both. "RIOT" is the version where the volume is up and the argument is direct.

We have been watching Lihtz build for years. "RIOT" is what the building looks like when the foundation is solid enough to support it.

posts.title: Superhuman
posts.writeup:
"Superhuman" is a word that describes what people who grew up in difficult circumstances are often asked to be: to absorb more, carry more, produce more, without the resources or support that would make any of it easier. Lihtz has been living in that reality since childhood, and the song treats the concept with the ambivalence it deserves. Being superhuman is not a compliment when it means not being allowed to be human.

His Universal Music Publishing deal came through the right doors: Future's attention opened a path, and Lihtz used it without losing what made him interesting in the first place. That is not a given. "Superhuman" is the work of someone who understood the assignment well enough to do it on his own terms.

258K monthly Spotify listeners for an independent artist with a difficult backstory and no conventional breakout single is its own kind of superpower. The catalog is building toward something.

posts.title: Visiting Hours
posts.writeup:
"Visiting Hours" was released on Valentine's Day 2025, which is either ironic or perfectly calculated, depending on how you read the title. Visiting hours are what you get when you are not allowed to be somewhere freely: a limited window, a time-constrained presence, a relationship with a fence around it. Lihtz has always written about constraint and what it does to people, and this track is a pointed version of that theme.

The release as a standalone single, separate from the larger 2025 project work, suggests it was ready before the rest: a statement that needed its own space rather than context. Lihtz understands pacing in his catalog in a way that some of his peers do not. He knows when something stands alone.

His West Philly origins and the difficult childhood he has described in interviews are not separate from what the music is doing. "Visiting Hours" is about access and limits, which are themes he has earned the right to write about.

posts.title: OUTBACK PARADISE
posts.writeup: "OUTBACK PARADISE" is SLOE JACK doing what he does with the production pushed a little harder than usual. There's an energy here that doesn't show up in everything he makes, and it suits the track. Another piece of a catalog that's starting to have real shape and weight to it.

posts.title: One Thing
posts.writeup:
"One Thing" is Lola Young in concentrated form: one idea, fully executed, with nothing wasted in the production or the performance. Her voice is a precise instrument, and she has learned to use it without overselling the moment. This track in particular benefits from that restraint: the more she holds back, the more the emotion accumulates, and by the time the song resolves, it has done its work.

The Greenwich, South London songwriter has been on a trajectory since "Messy" that her live shows and critical reception in 2025 have confirmed rather than created. She is an artist who performs at a level that makes people who see her live want to get everyone they know into the next show. "One Thing" is the recorded version of that experience.

We do not expect her to be an emerging artist for much longer. "One Thing" is the kind of song you put on a list of evidence.

posts.title: SMILE
posts.writeup:
"SMILE" closes the LETHAL tracklist, which gives it a specific structural function: it is the last word, the final image the album leaves you with. Rico Nasty choosing to close with a song called "SMILE" after a record full of the aggression, darkness, and intensity that defines the rest of LETHAL is a statement. It is not a softening. It is the face you make when you have said everything that needed to be said.

She is from the DMV (DC, Maryland, Virginia) corridor and has been building a catalog that defies easy categorization since she was a teenager posting on YouTube. LETHAL is the version of that catalog that arrived with a full label infrastructure behind it and still sounded like her rather than a produced version of her. "SMILE" is the punctuation.

The album got her the touring announcement and the coverage that a record like this demands. "SMILE" is the reason people stayed for the whole thing.

posts.title: Think About
posts.writeup:
Joe Boston, who records as Shallou, is a Los Angeles-based producer, singer, and environmentalist who has been building a catalog of ambient and downtempo electronic pop since around 2016. His music sits in the space that late-night listeners find: not quiet enough to be ambient strictly speaking, not loud enough to be dance music, just something that moves at the pace of thought. "Think About" is from his 2025 album 25, spring, a project that arrived in June and continued his practice of releasing music seasonally and with real intention about when it should be heard.

Boston has said he thinks of his music as environmental, both in the ecological sense (he is publicly committed to climate work) and in the architectural sense: sounds that build a space rather than occupy one. "Think About" fits that philosophy. The production is clean and layered without being busy, and the emotion in the track comes from what the arrangement holds back as much as what it pushes forward.

He has built a following of several million Spotify listeners without a major label infrastructure, which says something about what happens when the music is genuinely well-matched to the people who need it.

posts.title: she's all that i need
posts.writeup: woody writes small and hits hard. "she's all that i need" is understated folk-pop with a lyric that lands because it doesn't try too hard to land. His best work has this quality of sounding like you've heard it before, which is a compliment.

posts.title: Changer (feat. chlothegod)
posts.writeup:
Aminé keeps picking collaborators who have no business working together on paper but end up making total sense. For "Changer," he tapped chlothegod, the Fayetteville, North Carolina singer who built her following through open mics and bedroom recordings before landing a COLORS session and catching real attention in indie R&B circles. The combination works because both artists live comfortably in the in-between spaces: not quite rap, not quite R&B, not quite pop.

The track lands on the back half of 13 Months of Sunshine, Aminé's first solo album since 2020's Limbo. The whole record is him leaning into his Ethiopian and Eritrean roots, and "Changer" fits that ethos directly: the tempo drops, the raps get sharper, and chlothegod's presence gives the song a grounded, almost cinematic weight. She is not here for a hook. She is here as a counterforce.

We had been watching chlothegod quietly for a while before this. That this is the context she breaks through in makes total sense. Aminé has good ears, and "Changer" is the kind of deep cut that reveals exactly who both artists are when they are not trying to score a single.

posts.title: Doing The Best I Can
posts.writeup:
"Doing The Best I Can" opens with a voice note from Aminé's father. That decision sets the whole track before a note is played. This is a song about the weight of becoming the person your family needs you to be, written by someone who grew up the son of Ethiopian and Eritrean immigrants in Portland, Oregon, and spent the better part of a decade building a career that probably made very little sense to the people closest to him.

The song sits in the middle of 13 Months of Sunshine, Aminé's long-awaited third album. Where some of the record leans into loose summer energy and KAYTRAMINÉ-style bounce, this one slows down and asks something harder. Critics have compared it to the emotional reach of "Mama" from Limbo. We think that is right. It is the most exposed he has sounded in years.

Aminé has always been sharper than his comedic singles let on, and songs like this are where that clarity shows. The attempt at sounding unbothered everywhere else on the record makes this one hit harder when it arrives.

posts.title: History (feat. Waxahatchee)
posts.writeup:
The most surprising collaboration on 13 Months of Sunshine is not the one with The Streets or the one with 454. It is this one: Aminé and Katie Crutchfield, who records as Waxahatchee. Crutchfield is from Birmingham, Alabama, made her name in lo-fi folk-punk, and spent the last several years stepping into a more polished Americana lane with albums like Saint Cloud and Tigers Blood. What she is doing on a Portland rapper's album requires a moment of processing before it clicks completely.

Then it clicks completely. "History" works because Aminé has always had an ear for melody that runs closer to singer-songwriter territory than most of his peers. Crutchfield's voice and his coexist naturally here, like two people who have been writing in adjacent rooms for years without knowing it. The production holds space for both of them without flattening either.

We are always paying attention when genre fences get knocked down this casually. "History" is not a stunt. It is just a good song built by two people who trusted each other enough to make it.

posts.title: Sage Time
posts.writeup:
Track three on 13 Months of Sunshine, and one of the early signals that Aminé was making something with genuine range. "Sage Time" features Monsune and builds on the kind of easy, afrobeat-adjacent warmth that runs through the album's first act. It is the sort of track that does not announce itself but stays in rotation longer than the louder cuts.

Aminé came back after nearly five years away from a solo album. He spent that gap releasing the KAYTRAMINÉ project with Kaytranada, doing festival runs, and apparently spending real time thinking about what he wanted to say. The patience shows. "Sage Time" does not feel rushed or like it is proving anything. It just feels settled.

Portland to the bone, Ethiopian and Eritrean by lineage: Aminé has always written music that sits between those identities without explaining itself. This track is a good example of what that sounds like when it is working at its best.

posts.title: Drinking Til It Does
posts.writeup:
"Drinking Til It Does" is country songwriting at its most classic in terms of what it is doing emotionally: the attempt to use a substance to process something that requires actual processing. Wallen has written around this theme before, and it is not a romanticization. It is a recognition of how people actually behave, described with the specificity that makes it feel true rather than performed.

The track sits in the sequence of I'm the Problem as one of the harder-hitting emotional moments, not the loudest, but one of the most direct. The production does not over-insulate the writing from the listener. There is a roughness to the arrangement that keeps it honest.

Country has always had this relationship to drinking as both a coping mechanism and a narrative device, and the best songs in that tradition understand the difference between explaining a behavior and endorsing it. "Drinking Til It Does" knows the difference.

posts.title: Leavin's The Least I Could Do
posts.writeup:
The title does two things at once: it is the grammatically complete version of a difficult truth, and it is the kind of phrase that only makes sense after you have already accepted something that is hard to accept. "Leavin's The Least I Could Do" as a song title is country songwriting understanding itself. The leaving is not triumphant, it is just the minimum required action. That framing is better than most breakup songs manage.

Wallen has shown consistently that he knows when to let a lyric do its work without adding more on top of it. This track is built around that principle. The production is economical, the vocal is direct, and the emotional weight is in the writing rather than the performance. He sings it like someone who has already made the decision and is living in what comes after.

From a chart perspective, nearly everything Wallen releases goes to number one. From a craft perspective, tracks like this one are the reason the larger audience exists in the first place.

posts.title: Don't We
posts.writeup:
"Don't We" is the kind of title that lives in the uncertain space between a question and an answer. Morgan Wallen's best writing has always worked in that register: the country tradition of the song that asks the hard thing without being certain it wants to hear the reply. I'm the Problem carries a lot of that uncertainty through its track listing, and this one represents the more reflective end of the record.

Wallen grew up in a musical household in Sneedville, Tennessee, played guitar and piano from an early age, and came up through the American Idol path before finding his footing in Nashville. That background shapes how he writes: the melody-first instinct, the directness of the lyric, the vocal performance that earns the feeling rather than decorating it.

He has eleven number-ones on the Hot 100. The numbers are not the argument. The argument is that the songs consistently do what they set out to do. "Don't We" is a clean example.

posts.title: PROMISE
posts.writeup:
DAMEDAME* are Barbara Boko and Edith Nelson, an English duo who first made their name in the production world before stepping in front of the mic. They have production credits on Jorja Smith's sophomore album, and that background is audible: they understand how a song should sit in its own space, and "PROMISE" benefits from that structural intelligence at every level.

The track was released through Sony's Robots and Humans imprint in May 2025 and arrived as part of a run that established them as one of the more interesting propositions in UK R&B. Their take on the genre does not lean on familiar reference points. It pulls sideways and finds its own register.

We had been watching them in the production world for a while. Seeing what happens when they turn their attention fully inward has been one of the better things to follow this year.

posts.title: Burn Me
posts.writeup:
Jonah Kagen is a Georgia singer-songwriter signed to Arista Records who has been building a folk-adjacent catalog with consistent attention to craft. "Burn Me," written and recorded with Sam Barber, arrived in May 2025 as a single and tackled something specific: the internal experience of growing up around competing faiths, in a cultural environment where everyone has an opinion about how you are supposed to live.

The song came together on tour, which explains something about its energy. Road-written material tends to carry a different urgency than songs workshopped in studios. Both Kagen and Barber are writers first, and "Burn Me" reads that way: the performance serves the lyric rather than the reverse.

Barber is 2x platinum certified and brings real commercial credibility to this collaboration without pulling it in a more calculated direction. Kagen holds his own throughout. The combination produces something that lands in the emotional middle of what both artists do.

posts.title: CRASH
posts.writeup:
Rico Nasty has been one of the most individually distinctive voices in rap since her first viral moments around 2017, and LETHAL, her 2025 album via Fueled by Ramen and Atlantic Records, is her executing that distinctiveness within a framework that suits her better than her previous major label positioning did. She has said she dreamed of being somewhere with more space to grow. "CRASH" is the sound of that space being used.

The Maryland-born rapper's rap-rock hybrid is not a strategy. It is who she is: someone who grew up loving multiple things that the industry wants to keep separate, and who has been less interested in choosing between them than in figuring out how to be all of them at once. "CRASH" is that figuring-out at full volume.

Fueled by Ramen told her to stay true to herself. The label that broke Fall Out Boy into the mainstream letting a Maryland rapper do whatever she actually is: the outcome is exactly what you would hope it would be. "CRASH" earns its title.

posts.title: What I Want (feat. Tate McRae)
posts.writeup:
"What I Want" debuted at number one on the Billboard Hot 100, which made it Wallen's eighth song to open at the summit, extending his own record. For Tate McRae, it was her first visit to the country chart, which says something about the scale of what Wallen can do for a pop artist willing to cross over: he does not need country credibility points; he is the country chart.

Tate McRae is from Medicine Hat, Alberta, Canada, built a career through pop with genuine emotional depth, and her voice here sits with Wallen's in a way that surprised some listeners who did not expect the combination to work. It works because McRae has always written and performed with a directness that is closer to country's emotional tradition than most pop artists get. The crossover feels earned rather than engineered.

The song is about wanting something clearly, without qualifications, which is a deceptively difficult thing to write convincingly. Both of them sell it. The chart result followed accordingly.

posts.title: rose on a stone (1-800 GIRLS remix)
posts.writeup:
"rose on a stone" was already working when it came out in early 2025, produced by Ludwig A.F and Tom Flemming on BOXSET. The 1-800 GIRLS remix is the version that extended its reach. 1-800 GIRLS are an Australian production collective who have built a reputation for remixes that understand the original well enough to transform rather than merely adjust it.

just lil is an emerging artist with a small but deliberate catalog, and "rose on a stone" sits at the intersection of bedroom pop and something more cinematic. The remix adds texture and space without losing the intimacy that made the original worth remixing in the first place. That balance is harder to achieve than it sounds.

We pay attention to remix choices because they reveal something about how an artist understands their own work. Bringing in 1-800 GIRLS was a good call. The result is worth hearing in both versions.

posts.title: say you'll never leave
posts.writeup:
"say you'll never leave" is built on a request that the song itself seems to know is already lost. jigitz is an emerging artist whose work sits in a lo-fi R&B space, and this track operates from that tension between wanting to hold something and knowing you probably cannot. The production is hazy and close-mic'd, which is the right choice: a high-gloss arrangement would work against the intimacy the writing is trying to create.

The lowercase aesthetic and the unpunctuated title are consistent signals: this is an artist who is not interested in performing confidence. The emotion in the song is genuine, and the understated presentation serves it.

jigitz is still in the early stages of building a catalog, which means the data is thin and the audience is small. That is not a reason to look away. It is the reason we pay attention at this stage.

posts.title: dog at the door
posts.writeup:
ken xox is a singer-songwriter working in the space between folk-pop and indie-alternative, and "dog at the door" arrived in May 2025 as a standalone single. The title image is domestic and specific, and the song earns it: the dog at the door is a real image before it becomes a metaphorical one, and the writing keeps that grounded quality throughout.

She has been releasing material at a careful pace since 2024, with songs like "Prayed Like Hell" and "Walk Away" building a catalog that rewards close attention. The production on "dog at the door" is clean and warm, with space around the vocal that lets the writing do its work. Nothing is buried or over-decorated.

ken xox is in the early stages of an interesting career. "dog at the door" is the kind of single that arrives without fanfare and ends up on repeat. We notice those.

posts.title: Is It Real (feat. Ravyn Lenae)
posts.writeup:
Flume and JPEGMAFIA put out the We Live in a Society EP together in May 2025, and "Is It Real" is its most fully realized track. Flume is the Sydney-born producer who redefined what Australian electronic music could be on an international stage; JPEGMAFIA is the Baltimore-raised, Brooklyn-based artist who has spent the last several years making experimental rap records that refuse to sit still. Together they share an appreciation for chaos that resolves into something genuinely musical.

Ravyn Lenae's presence on "Is It Real" is the variable that makes the track land differently than the rest of the EP. Lenae, the Chicago singer who has been building one of the more careful R&B catalogs of the past few years, brings a melodic grounding that contrasts with everything the production is doing around her. It is not a comfortable combination. That is the point.

We expected this collaboration to be interesting on paper. Hearing it is something else. "Is It Real" is three artists operating at the edge of what makes sense and trusting each other to land it.

posts.title: Man of the Year
posts.writeup:
Sam Barber was 21 years old when "Man of the Year" came out in April 2025, and he wrote it entirely himself. The song opens with him taking stock of despair: the kind around him, the kind inside him. "Is it in my mind, or are we all just sinking?" is a question a 21-year-old songwriter from what has been described as a small-town Tennessee background writes when he is not pretending the world is fine. The song does not pretend the world is fine.

Barber has a 2x platinum certification behind him already, which means the audience for what he does is already substantial. "Man of the Year" is him stepping toward something more introspective than previous singles: the title is ironic, the song is earnest, and the gap between those two things is where it lives.

Country needs young writers who are not afraid of the harder questions, and Barber has shown consistently that he is not. "Man of the Year" is a strong example of why he is worth paying close attention to.

posts.title: Germaphobe
posts.writeup:
Provoker are an American post-punk and darkwave act whose 2025 double single "Germaphobe / Tears in the Club" arrived with the kind of cold wave economy that genre demands: minimal production, clear intent, no wasted motion. "Germaphobe" is the more physically driven of the two, built on a rhythm section that operates like anxiety made percussive, with a vocal that stays on the surface of the emotion rather than diving through it.

Post-punk has had consistent revival cycles, but Provoker's approach is less retro than that framing suggests. They are working in a tradition that has never fully stopped being relevant and updating it without announcing the update. "Germaphobe" is not a 1980s pastiche. It is a 2025 song with those roots visible but not foregrounded.

The double-single format is smart for this material: "Germaphobe" and "Tears in the Club" do different things and set up the full album effectively. We pulled this one because the energy is real and the execution is precise.

posts.title: Com Amor Bahia
posts.writeup:
Juls is British-Ghanaian, based in London, and has been one of the more interesting producers working in the space where Afrobeats and global sounds intersect since breaking through with Wizkid collaborations and the 2017 track "Bad." "Com Amor Bahia," featuring Brazilian singer Luedji Luna, is from his Com Amor Brazil EP, a project where he crossed the Atlantic musically and came back with something that sounds like it belongs to both places equally.

Luedji Luna is from Bahia, Brazil, and her voice carries that geographic and cultural rootedness. The pairing with Juls is not obvious, but it works precisely because both artists understand rhythm as a foundation rather than a garnish. The production is spare enough to give Luna room, and the result is a track that operates more as a conversation between two traditions than as a fusion of them.

Juls has consistently found collaborators who meet him where he is rather than where the market says they should be. "Com Amor Bahia" is one of the more direct examples of what that looks like.

posts.title: Loser
posts.writeup:
Julia Wolf is from Queens, lives in Los Angeles, and has been building a catalog since her 2021 debut EP Girls in Purgatory that draws on gothic pop and late-2000s indie nostalgia in ways that sound current rather than retro. By 2025 her profile had grown considerably: a Drake feature, a Vice profile calling out her Twilight-era aesthetic and Caroline Polachek comparison, and a second album called Pressure out via AWAL.

"Loser" was the lead single from Pressure and sets the tone for what the album is doing: the self-deprecating title is doing some work, but the song does not actually believe what it is saying about itself. Wolf has always written with a tension between vulnerability and defiance, and "Loser" is a clean example of what that sounds like when the production and delivery are aligned.

She has been one of the more interesting indie pop artists to watch in the first half of this decade. Pressure is her making that argument at full volume.

posts.title: Same Way - Twitch Tapes
posts.writeup: Tory Lanez from the Twitch Tapes era catching him in a looser, more spontaneous mode. "Same Way" has a rawness that his more polished output doesn't always allow, the vocal less controlled and more present for it. A different angle on an artist with a lot of them.

posts.title: Alone
posts.writeup:
"Alone" is the kind of title that carries all the weight the song needs before it starts. frank, the artist behind this, is working in a space where the production is spare enough that the writing has nowhere to hide. The vocal is close, the arrangement is minimal, and the central question the song asks is not answered by the time it ends. That is a choice, and it is the right one.

Early-career recordings often carry a directness that more polished work tends to sand down. "Alone" has that quality: the rawness feels like information rather than limitation. There is a sense that this is exactly the version of the song that needed to exist, not a rougher draft of something better.

We track artists in moments like this because the gap between where they are and where they are going is often visible. With frank, what is already on the table is worth paying attention to.

posts.title: Decay
posts.writeup: STELLA LEFTY has a voice that carries weight without pushing for it. "Decay" is sparse and direct, the production staying out of the way while she does the work. There's a confidence in the restraint here. Keep an ear on where she goes next.

posts.title: Vacay
posts.writeup:
"Vacay" is the unambiguous summer moment on 13 Months of Sunshine, and it earns that position. Featuring Yame, it runs barely over two minutes, packs in one of Aminé's most animated verses, and lands a hook that does not overstay its welcome. Reviews called it one of the album's standout moments, which tracks: it is built for immediate, total comprehension.

Aminé dropped this album in May 2025 after a nearly five-year gap in solo releases. Between Limbo and this record, he put out KAYTRAMINÉ with Kaytranada and maintained a steady touring presence. But returning with a full solo statement clearly required care about what that statement should be, and "Vacay" represents the confident, uncomplicated end of that calculation.

We appreciate albums that know when to stop explaining themselves and just let something be fun. "Vacay" is that track on this project. It serves exactly one purpose and does it well.

posts.title: LILAC HUES
posts.writeup:
"LILAC HUES" is the closing track on Lapsley's third album I'm a Hurricane I'm a Woman in Love, and as a closer it does what the title suggests: softer, more blurred, the album settling into something that has processed all the energy that came before it. Holly Lapsley Fletcher is from Southport, England, signed to XL Recordings at 19, and has spent a decade developing a production sensibility that has moved from glacial electronica toward something warmer and more structurally complex.

The album title is its own emotional claim: a hurricane is force without control, and love as Fletcher describes it does not behave. "LILAC HUES" is what remains when the hurricane has passed. The production is close and soft, the vocal sits in a different relationship to the arrangement than on the louder tracks, and the effect is one of earned quiet.

She sounds nothing like who she was on Long Way Home in 2016, and everything like a natural development from that record. "LILAC HUES" is where that development arrives.

posts.title: NORTH FACE
posts.writeup:
"NORTH FACE" is track ten on I'm a Hurricane I'm a Woman in Love, one step before the closer, and it carries the weight of someone who has been inside the record long enough to see what it is building toward. The reference to North Face, the outdoor apparel brand associated with cold weather and protection, is the kind of image Lapsley uses well: something functional that becomes emotional, an object that carries a feeling without announcing it.

She has always written about weather as emotional infrastructure: wind, cold, and warmth doing the work that more direct language would labor over. "NORTH FACE" fits in that tradition. The production is full and deliberate, and her voice here is at its most confident placement in the record's architecture.

The album as a whole was a critical statement of where she is as an artist at 28, a decade into a career that started when she was still a teenager. "NORTH FACE" is a strong argument for the whole project.

posts.title: Is It Real (feat. Ravyn Lenae)
posts.writeup:
Flume and JPEGMAFIA put out the We Live in a Society EP together in May 2025, and "Is It Real" is its most fully realized track. Flume is the Sydney-born producer who redefined what Australian electronic music could be on an international stage; JPEGMAFIA is the Baltimore-raised, Brooklyn-based artist who has spent the last several years making experimental rap records that refuse to sit still. Together they share an appreciation for chaos that resolves into something genuinely musical.

Ravyn Lenae's presence on "Is It Real" is the variable that makes the track land differently than the rest of the EP. Lenae, the Chicago singer who has been building one of the more careful R&B catalogs of the past few years, brings a melodic grounding that contrasts with everything the production is doing around her. It is not a comfortable combination. That is the point.

We expected this collaboration to be interesting on paper. Hearing it is something else. "Is It Real" is three artists operating at the edge of what makes sense and trusting each other to land it.

posts.title: WE DID IT
posts.writeup:
DONDA is the Los Angeles-based creative collective that Kanye West formally organized around the time of his 2021 album of the same name. They have operated across music, fashion, architecture, and design, functioning as a loose creative infrastructure more than a traditional music project. "WE DID IT" arrives in that context: a track that carries the energy and weight of the whole enterprise.

The production is maximalist and confrontational, which is the house style for anything coming out of that camp. Whether you read it as genuine triumph or as something more complicated, the song commits fully to its premise. There is no hedging here. The confidence, whatever you make of its source, is at full volume.

We note the track and the collective because both are worth tracking, independent of whatever your current feelings are about the broader Kanye situation. The music does what it sets out to do.

posts.title: RIDE THE SMOKE
posts.writeup: The title alone sets the tempo. "RIDE THE SMOKE" has that slow-building SLOE JACK quality where the hook doesn't announce itself, it just arrives. The production is patient and the payoff is earned. Three cuts in the catalog now and the through line is clear: this artist has a real voice.

posts.title: The Gathering
